etymology.description: 等(等) (děng, "wait; rank; kind; etc.") is repeated to form a phrase that suggests the idea of continuation or a list of similar items.
etymology.interpretation: By repeating 等(等) (děng, "wait; rank; kind; etc."), the character emphasizes the idea of continuation or that there are more similar items yet unlisted. The concept of postponing the conclusion or extending a category logically leads to 'et cetera' as it implies the presence of further similar elements. Originally, the meaning comes from ranking or classifying, extending to list continuation by implying additional elements are of the same rank or kind.

examples.chinese: 商店里有各种水果，比如苹果(蘋果)、香蕉(香蕉)、橙子(橙子)等等(等等)。
examples.pinyin: Shāngdiàn lǐ yǒu gèzhǒng shuǐguǒ, bǐrú píngguǒ, xiāngjiāo, chéngzi děngděng.
examples.english: The store has various fruits, such as apples, bananas, oranges, etc.

examples.chinese: 他擅长多种乐器，比如钢琴(鋼琴)、吉他(吉他)、小提琴(小提琴)等等(等等)。
examples.pinyin: Tā shàncháng duōzhǒng yuèqì, bǐrú gāngqín, jítā, xiǎotíqín děngděng.
examples.english: He is good at various musical instruments, such as the piano, guitar, violin, etc.

examples.chinese: 温室里种植了西红柿(西紅柿)、黄瓜(黃瓜)、胡萝卜(胡蘿蔔)等等(等等)。
examples.pinyin: Wēnshì lǐ zhòngzhí le xīhóngshì, huángguā, húluóbo děngděng.
examples.english: The greenhouse grows tomatoes, cucumbers, carrots, etc.
